7.1: How beautiful are your feet in sandals, O noble daughter! Your graceful thighs are like jeweled ornaments, crafted by a skilled artisan.
7.2: Your navel is like a rounded goblet that never lacks spiced wine; your belly is like a mound of wheat encircled with lilies.
7.3: Your two breasts are like two fawns, twins of a gazelle.
7.4: Your neck is like a tower of ivory; your eyes are like the pools of Heshbon beside the gate of Bath-rabbim; your nose is like the tower of Lebanon that looks out toward Damascus.
7.5: Your head crowns you like Mount Carmel, and your flowing hair is like royal purple; a king is held captive in your tresses.
7.6: How beautiful you are and how pleasant, O love, in your delights!
7.7: Your stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters of dates.
7.8: I said, 'I will climb the palm tree and take hold of its boughs.' May your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples.
7.9: And your kisses like the best wine — flowing smoothly for my beloved, gliding gently over sleeping lips.
7.10: I am my beloved's, and his desire is for me.
7.11: Come, my beloved, let us go out to the fields and spend the night in the villages.
7.12: Let us rise early and go to the vineyards to see whether the vine has budded, whether the blossoms have opened, and whether the pomegranates are in bloom. There I will give you my love.
7.13: The mandrakes give forth their fragrance, and at our doors are all choice fruits, both new and old, which I have kept in store for you, my beloved.

8.1: If only you were like a brother to me, one who nursed at my mother's breasts! If I found you in the street I would kiss you, and no one would look down on me.
8.2: I would lead you and bring you into my mother's house, where she would instruct me. I would give you spiced wine to drink, the nectar of my pomegranate.
8.3: His left hand is under my head, and his right hand embraces me.
8.4: I charge you, O daughters of Jerusalem: do not arouse or awaken love until it is ready.
8.5: Who is this coming up from the wilderness, leaning on her beloved? Under the apple tree I awakened you; there your mother was in labor with you; there she who bore you labored in birth.
8.6: Set me as a seal upon your heart, as a seal upon your arm; for love is as strong as death, jealousy as fierce as Sheol; its flashes are flashes of fire, the most vehement flame.
8.7: Many waters cannot quench love, and rivers cannot drown it. If a man offered all the wealth of his house in exchange for love, he would be utterly despised.
8.8: We have a little sister, and she has no breasts as yet. What shall we do for our sister on the day when she is spoken for in marriage?
8.9: If she is a wall, we will build on her a parapet of silver; but if she is a door, we will enclose her with panels of cedar.
8.10: I am a wall, and my breasts are like towers; so I have become in his eyes as one who brings peace.
8.11: Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon; he entrusted it to tenant keepers; each one was to bring a thousand pieces of silver for its fruit.
8.12: My very own vineyard is my own; the thousand is yours, O Solomon, and two hundred for those who tend its fruit.
8.13: O you who dwell in the gardens, while companions are listening for your voice, let me hear you.
8.14: Make haste, my beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young stag upon the mountains of spices.

1.1: The Song of Songs, by Solomon.
1.2: Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth! For your love is better than wine.
1.3: Your anointing oils are fragrant; your name is like perfume poured out; no wonder the young women love you.
1.4: Take me with you — let us run! The king has brought me into his chambers. We will rejoice and exult in you; we will celebrate your love more than wine. Rightly do they love you!
1.5: I am dark and lovely, O daughters of Jerusalem — like the tents of Kedar, like the draperies of Solomon.
1.6: Do not stare at me because I am dark — the sun has beaten down on me. My brothers were angry and made me keeper of the vineyards; my own vineyard I have not tended.
1.7: Tell me, you whom I love with all my heart, where you pasture your flock and where you rest them at midday — why should I be left wandering among the flocks of your companions?
1.8: If you do not know, O most beautiful of women, follow the tracks of the flock and pasture your young goats near the shepherds' tents.
1.9: I liken you, my love, to a mare harnessed to Pharaoh's chariots.
1.10: Your cheeks are beautiful with jewelry, your neck adorned with strings of beads.
1.11: We will make for you gold ornaments studded with silver.
1.12: While the king reclined at his table, my perfume spread its fragrance.
1.13: My beloved is to me a sachet of myrrh resting between my breasts.
1.14: My beloved is to me a cluster of henna blossoms from the vineyards of En-gedi.
1.15: How beautiful you are, my love! How beautiful! Your eyes are like doves.
1.16: How handsome you are, my beloved, how truly delightful! Our bed is a bower of green.
1.17: The beams of our house are cedar; our rafters are cypress.

2.1: I am a wildflower of Sharon, a lily of the valleys.
2.2: Like a lily among thorns, so is my beloved among the young women.
2.3: Like an apple tree among the trees of the forest, so is my beloved among men. With great delight I sat in his shade, and his fruit was sweet to my taste.
2.4: He brought me to the banquet hall, and his banner over me was love.
2.5: Strengthen me with raisins; revive me with apples — for I am faint with love.
2.6: His left arm is under my head, and his right arm holds me close.
2.7: I implore you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and the does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it is ready.
2.8: Listen — it is my beloved! Here he comes, leaping across the mountains, bounding over the hills.
2.9: My beloved is like a gazelle or a young stag. There he stands behind our wall, peering through the windows, looking through the lattice.
2.10: My beloved spoke and said to me: 'Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me.'
2.11: 'The winter is past; the rains are over and gone.'
2.12: 'Flowers appear all across the land; the season of singing has arrived, and the voice of the turtledove is heard in our countryside.'
2.13: 'The fig tree is ripening its early fruit, and the blossoming vines spread their fragrance. Get up, my love, my beautiful one, and come with me.'
2.14: 'My dove, hidden in the clefts of the rock, in the sheltered crevice of the cliff — let me see your face and hear your voice. Your voice is sweet and your face is beautiful.'
2.15: 'Catch the little foxes that ruin the vineyards — our vineyards are in bloom.'
2.16: My beloved is mine and I am his; he grazes among the lilies.
2.17: Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, turn, my beloved — be like a gazelle or a young stag on the rugged mountains.

3.1: At night on my bed I searched for the one I love with all my heart; I searched for him but did not find him.
3.2: 'I will get up and go about the city — through the streets and squares I will search for the one I love.' I searched but did not find him.
3.3: The city guards found me as they made their rounds: 'Have you seen the one I love?'
3.4: I had barely passed them when I found the one I love. I held him and would not let him go until I brought him into my mother's house, into the room of the one who gave birth to me.
3.5: I implore you, daughters of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and the does of the field: do not rouse or awaken love until it is ready.
3.6: Who is this coming up from the desert like a column of smoke, fragrant with myrrh and frankincense, with every kind of spice from the trader?
3.7: It is the royal litter of Solomon, surrounded by sixty warriors — the finest men of Israel.
3.8: All of them carry swords and are skilled in battle, each with his sword at his side, ready for danger in the night.
3.9: King Solomon made his royal litter from Lebanese cedar.
3.10: Its posts are silver, its back of gold, its seat purple — its interior lovingly lined by the daughters of Jerusalem.
3.11: Come out, daughters of Zion, and gaze on King Solomon wearing the crown with which his mother crowned him — on his wedding day, the day his heart rejoiced.

4.1: How beautiful you are, my love! How beautiful! Your eyes behind your veil are doves. Your hair is like a flock of goats cascading down the slopes of Gilead.
4.2: Your teeth are like a flock of freshly washed sheep, each with its twin — not one is missing.
4.3: Your lips are like a scarlet thread, and your mouth is lovely. Your cheeks are like the halves of a pomegranate behind your veil.
4.4: Your neck is like the tower of David, built in courses of stone; a thousand shields hang on it, all of them warriors' shields.
4.5: Your two breasts are like two young fawns, twins of a gazelle, feeding among the lilies.
4.6: Until the day breaks and the shadows disappear, I will go to the mountain of myrrh and the hill of frankincense.
4.7: You are altogether beautiful, my love; there is no defect in you.
4.8: Come with me from Lebanon, my bride; come from Lebanon. Come down from the peak of Amana, from the summit of Senir and Hermon, from the dens of lions, from the mountain haunts of leopards.
4.9: You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride; you have stolen my heart with a single glance, with one jewel of your necklace.
4.10: How beautiful your love is, my sister, my bride! How much better is your love than wine, and the fragrance of your perfumes than any spice!
4.11: Your lips, my bride, drip with nectar; honey and milk are under your tongue; the fragrance of your garments is like the fragrance of Lebanon.
4.12: My sister, my bride, is a garden locked, a spring sealed, a fountain closed.
4.13: Your branches are an orchard of pomegranates with every choice fruit — henna and nard.
4.14: Nard and saffron, calamus and cinnamon, with all trees of frankincense — myrrh and aloes, with all the finest spices.
4.15: A garden fountain, a well of fresh water, streams flowing from Lebanon.
4.16: Awake, O north wind, and come, O south wind! Blow through my garden and carry its fragrance abroad. Let my beloved come to his garden and eat its best fruits.

5.1: I have come to my garden, my sister, my bride; I gathered my myrrh with my spice. I ate my honeycomb with my honey; I drank my wine with my milk. Eat, friends; drink deeply — be drunk with love!
5.2: I slept, but my heart was awake. A voice — my beloved knocking: 'Open to me, my sister, my darling, my dove, my perfect one. My head is drenched with dew, my hair with the drops of the night.'
5.3: 'I have taken off my robe — how can I put it back on? I have washed my feet — how can I dirty them again?'
5.4: My beloved thrust his hand through the latch opening, and I felt a deep longing for him.
5.5: I got up to open to my beloved, and my hands dripped with myrrh — my fingers with flowing myrrh — on the handles of the lock.
5.6: I opened the door to my beloved, but my beloved had gone. I was beside myself when he left. I searched but did not find him; I called but he did not answer.
5.7: The city guards found me as they made their rounds; they beat me and wounded me; the watchmen on the walls stripped off my shawl.
5.8: I implore you, daughters of Jerusalem: if you find my beloved, tell him I am faint with love.
5.9: 'What makes your beloved better than any other lover, O most beautiful of women? What makes him so special that you beg us this way?'
5.10: My beloved is radiant and rugged, outstanding among ten thousand men.
5.11: His head is like purest gold; his hair is wavy and dark as a raven.
5.12: His eyes are like doves beside streams of water — bathed in milk, perfectly set.
5.13: His cheeks are like beds of spice, banks of sweet herbs. His lips are lilies dripping with flowing myrrh.
5.14: His hands are gold cylinders set with jewels. His torso is carved ivory overlaid with sapphires.
5.15: His legs are like columns of alabaster, set in sockets of gold. His form is like Lebanon — magnificent as the cedars.
5.16: His mouth is sweetness itself; he is altogether desirable. This is my beloved and this is my friend, O daughters of Jerusalem.

6.1: 'Where has your beloved gone, O most beautiful of women? Which way did he go? We will search with you.'
6.2: My beloved has gone to his garden, to the beds of spices, to graze in the gardens and gather lilies.
6.3: I am my beloved's and he is mine; he grazes among the lilies.
6.4: You are beautiful as Tirzah, my love, lovely as Jerusalem, awe-inspiring as an army under banners.
6.5: Look away — your eyes overwhelm me! Your hair flows like a flock of goats down from Gilead.
6.6: Your teeth are like a flock of sheep that have just been washed — every one bearing twins, none having lost her young.
6.7: Your cheeks are like pomegranate halves behind your veil.
6.8: There may be sixty queens and eighty concubines, and maidens beyond counting.
6.9: But my dove, my flawless one, is unique — her mother's only daughter, treasured by the one who gave birth to her. The young women saw her and praised her; the queens and concubines celebrated her.
6.10: 'Who is this who appears like the dawn, beautiful as the moon, bright as the sun, awe-inspiring as an army with banners?'
6.11: I went down to the grove of walnut trees to look at the new growth in the valley, to see whether the vines had budded or the pomegranates bloomed.
6.12: Before I realized it, my longing had placed me beside my noble kinsman's chariot.
6.13: Come back, come back, O Shulamite; come back, come back, so we can look at you! 'Why do you gaze on the Shulamite as at the dance between two companies?'
